label: Topology
definition: The study of the properties of shapes and spaces that survive continuous deformation such as stretching and bending but not tearing or gluing, formalizing nearness without relying on distance.

body.text: Topology is geometry stripped down to what survives continuous deformation. It studies the properties of a space that do not change when you stretch, bend, or twist it, as long as you never tear it or glue distinct points together. To a topologist a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same object, because each has exactly one hole and one can be continuously molded into the other; a sphere and a doughnut are genuinely different, because no continuous deformation can remove or create a hole. The formal device that makes this rigorous is the topological space, a set together with a designated family of open sets satisfying simple axioms. Open sets encode the notion of nearness directly, without any numerical distance, which is what lets topology apply even where no sensible metric exists.

With nearness defined by open sets, the central concepts follow. A continuous map is one whose preimages of open sets are open, the abstract version of a map with no sudden jumps. A homeomorphism is a continuous map with a continuous inverse, the precise statement that two spaces are topologically identical. The properties topology cares about are the ones preserved by homeomorphism, the topological invariants: connectedness (is the space in one piece), compactness (a finiteness condition that generalizes being closed and bounded), and the structure of holes in various dimensions. These invariants are what let you prove two spaces are different by exhibiting a property one has and the other lacks.

Algebraic topology makes the counting of holes computable by attaching algebraic objects to a space, most famously the homology and homotopy groups, which turn geometric features into group-theory data that a computer can manipulate. The fundamental group records loops that cannot be contracted to a point; higher homology records higher-dimensional voids. This translation from shape to algebra is one of the great achievements of twentieth-century mathematics and is the engine behind the data-analysis applications below. Topology also supplies the setting in which a manifold is defined: a manifold is a topological space that looks locally like ordinary flat space, and the smooth structure that differential-geometry adds sits on top of this topological skeleton.

In machine learning, topology shows up wherever the shape of data matters more than its exact coordinates. The manifold-hypothesis, the claim that high-dimensional data concentrates near a low-dimensional manifold, is fundamentally topological: it asserts the data has a simple intrinsic shape hidden inside a complicated ambient space. Dimensionality-reduction and manifold-learning methods try to recover that shape and unfold it into a flat representation, which is sound only when they respect the data's topology, preserving which points are near which and how the pieces connect. When a method tears or wrongly glues the manifold, it produces a misleading latent-space.

Topological data analysis turns these ideas into a concrete toolkit. Persistent homology examines a data set across all scales at once, recording which holes and connected components appear and how long they persist as you thicken the cloud of points, and summarizes the result in a persistence diagram that is robust to noise and invariant to rotation and stretching. This yields features that capture global structure, like loops and voids in the data, that distance-based summaries miss entirely. Topology also clarifies why certain tasks are hard: a classifier built from continuous layers cannot change the topology of its input without help, so separating classes that are topologically entangled forces the representation to first disentangle them. Across these uses, topology is the language for the qualitative shape of data, complementing the quantitative, distance-based view of geometry.
